q: In 1678, the Dutch physicist Christiaan Huygens introduced the wave theory of light.
Huygens' principle states that every point on a wavefront may be considered as a source of secondary wavelets.
The new position of a wavefront is the envelope of the wavelets.
Secondary wavelets will have a different frequency and speed than the propagating wave.
Secondary wavelets spread out in all directions with the speed $v$.
A wavefront is defined as a surface associated with a propagating wave which is a locus of all points in the wave that have the same phase.
On a particular wavefront, at a given moment of time, all particles of the medium are undergoing different phases.
Wavefront is always parallel to the direction of propagation (or) rays.
There are two types of wavefront: plane wavefront and spherical wavefront.
The spherical wavefront from a source transforms into plane wavefront when it is far away from the source.
Wave optics includes the wave characteristics of light.
Physical optics is another term for wave optics.
The study of interference and diffraction of light is dealt with wave optics.
Huygens' principle explained how the light propagates from a light source.
The secondary wavelets have the same frequency $f$ and speed $v$ as the original wave.
The angular position of a point of a periodic wave is known as the phase angle.
The phase angle is the position of a point on a reference circle of a wavefront at an instant.
The phase angle is one of the important characteristics of the periodic wave.
If phase difference are $0, 2\pi, 4\pi, 6\pi, \dots$, these two positions are in phase (same phase).
If phase difference are $\pi, 3\pi, 5\pi, \dots$, these two positions are completely out of phase.
Path difference indicates the distance between two points measured along the direction of propagation of the wave through the medium.
If path difference are $0, \lambda, 2\lambda, 3\lambda, \dots$, it is said to be completely out of phase.
If path difference are $\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{3\lambda}{2}, \frac{5\lambda}{2}, \dots$, it is said to be in phase.
The relation between phase difference and path difference is expressed as $\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta x$.
When a plane wave passes through a slit, a circular wavelet emerges from the slit according to Huygens' principle.
In Young's double slits experiment, each slit acts as a secondary source of circular waves.
Two waves are coherent if they are of same frequency, same amplitude and are in phase.
The interference pattern consists of a series of bright and dark parallel lines called fringes.
Constructive interference occurs at the centre of the screen because waves travel the same distance and are in phase.
A crest of one wave arriving at the same time as a crest of the other wave results in constructive interference.
Light waves from two sources $S_1$ and $S_2$ are initially in phase in Young's experiment.
If the path difference is equal to an integral number of wavelengths $(0, \lambda, 2\lambda, \dots)$, the waves will interfere constructively.
If the path difference is equal to a half-integral number of wavelengths $(\frac{\lambda}{2}, \frac{3\lambda}{2}, \frac{5\lambda}{2}, \dots)$, the waves will interfere destructively.
Constructive interference results in bright fringes and destructive interference results in a dark fringe.
The position of particular bright and dark fringes on the screen is given by the distance from the central bright fringe, denoted $y$.
Path difference for bright fringes is expressed as $d \sin \theta = m\lambda$, where $m = 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
The variable $m$ in the condition for bright fringes is called the order of bright fringe.
The zeroth order ($m = 0$) bright corresponds to the central bright line at $\theta = 0$.
The first-order brights ($m = 1$) can be seen on only one side of the central line.
The condition for dark fringes is given by $d \sin \theta = (m + \frac{1}{2})\lambda$ for $m = 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
Distance between two adjacent bright (or dark) fringes is called the fringe width.
Fringe width is given by the formula $\Delta y = \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
The position of fringes and fringe width depend on the wavelength of light source.
In Young's double slit experiment, bright fringes have maximum intensity and intensity of dark fringe is zero.
Bright and dark fringes are formed alternatively in Young's double slit experiment.
Monochromatic light means that it has a single wavelength.
Interference of light waves is defined as the modification in the distribution of light energy (intensity) when two or more waves superimpose each other.
Coherent sources have the same frequency and constant phase difference.
The condition for constructive interference is that the path difference should be equal to an integral multiple of the wavelength.
The condition for destructive interference is that the path difference should be equal to odd integral multiple of half wavelength.
A crest of one wave arriving at the same time as a trough of the other wave results in destructive interference.
The distance of the $m^{th}$ bright fringe from the central bright line is $y = m \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
The distance of the $m^{th}$ dark fringe from the central bright line is $y = (m + \frac{1}{2}) \frac{\lambda D}{d}$.
If the waves emitted from two sources have the same frequency and constant phase difference, the sources are coherent.
The position of fringes depends on the distance between the slits $d$.
The fringe width increases if the wavelength of the light source increases.
The distance $D$ between the slits and the screen affects the fringe width.
The path difference for the central bright fringe is equal to zero.
In Young's experiment, the waves from the two slits travel different distances to points other than the center.
Intensity distribution for Young's fringes shows that intensity varies with path difference.
Diffraction is defined as the bending of waves around the corners of an obstacle (or) through an aperture.
The amount of bending depends on the wavelength of light relative to the size of the aperture.
If the aperture is much larger than the wavelength of light, the bending will be very noticeable.
Light waves diffract around objects and create bright and dark fringes.
In Fresnel diffraction, the source of light and the screen are at a finite distance from the obstacle causing the diffraction.
In Fraunhofer diffraction, there is a finite distance between the source and the screen from the obstacle.
The experimental arrangement for single slit diffraction usually involves Fraunhofer diffraction.
When light passes through a single slit whose width $a$ is of the order of the wavelength of light, a diffraction pattern is formed.
The diffraction pattern consists of a bright central portion called the central maximum.
On either side of the central maximum, there are alternating bright and dark fringes.
Each successive bright fringe in a diffraction pattern becomes more intense proceeding outward.
Secondary wavelets from the upper and lower halves of a single slit reach the center point in phase to produce the central bright maximum.
The first minimum is formed when waves from the centre and top (or centre and bottom) of the slit have a path difference of $\frac{\lambda}{2}$.
For the first minimum in single slit diffraction, the condition is $a \sin \theta = \lambda$.
For the $m^{th}$ minimum in single-slit diffraction, the condition is $a \sin \theta = m\lambda$ where $m = 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
The width of the central maximum in single-slit diffraction is equal to twice the position of the first minimum.
The width of the central maximum is expressed as $2 \frac{\lambda D}{a}$.
The width of the central maximum depends on the width of the slit.
A narrower slit causes a narrower central maximum in a diffraction pattern.
Successive bright and dark fringes in single-slit diffraction are created by interference of diffracted light waves.
Diffraction occurs into the region of geometrical shadow of an obstacle.
Intensity distribution for single-slit diffraction shows the highest intensity at the central maximum.
A diffraction grating is an optical device which consists of a large number of parallel slits of same width separated by equal opaque spaces.
A diffraction grating can disperse light into its wavelength components.
Diffraction grating is used for the purpose of resolving light into spectra.
Diffraction grating is widely used as dispersive elements in spectrographic instruments.
The two types of grating are transmission grating and reflection grating.
A transmission grating can be manufactured by carving glass with a sharp tool in a large number of precisely positioned parallel lines.
The number of lines (or slits) per unit length is called the grating constant.
The condition for maximum intensity in a diffraction grating is the same as that for a Young's double slit.
For maximum intensity to be formed at $P$ in a grating, the path difference of two waves from adjacent slits is equal to an integral number of wavelengths.
The grating equation for maxima is $d \sin \theta = m\lambda$, where $m = 0, 1, 2, 3, \dots$.
In the grating equation, $d$ is the distance between the adjacent slits.
If the number of slits per unit length is $N$, then the distance $d$ is equal to $1/N$.
In a diffraction spectrum, there is no dispersion of incident white light at the centre $(m = 0)$.
In the first order diffraction spectrum, red light (longer wavelength) diffracts at a greater angle than violet light (shorter wavelength).
A compact disc is an example of reflection gratings.
The tracks of a compact disc act as a diffraction grating, producing a separation of the colors of white light.
A reflection grating is a shiny surface having many fine grooves.
Diffraction grating is a crucial tool in the study of atomic and molecular compositions.
A grating containing several thousand slits per centimetre is used for practical purposes.
The condition for maximum intensity involves an integral number of wavelengths.
